evidence_quotes.quote: pass these costs on to users through subscriptions, ads, or in-app purchases
If developers must pay fees to Google to publish their apps, they may pass these costs on to users through subscriptions, ads, or in-app purchases.

paragraphs.text: Concerns about restricting Android app distribution to the Google Play Store Reduced consumer choice and digital freedom Today, Android users benefit from sideloading and alternative app stores, enabling them to install open-source, niche, or region-specific applications. Forcing all app distribution through Google Play would remove this flexibility, locking consumers into a single, corporate-controlled channel. Barrier to open-source innovation Many open-source developers contribute applications for free and without profit motive. If Google requires developers to go through an approval process possibly with fees or complex bureaucracy these projects may no longer be viable. This would shrink the ecosystem of non-commercial apps, depriving consumers of privacy-friendly and community-driven alternatives. Risk of higher costs for consumers If developers must pay fees to Google to publish their apps, they may pass these costs on to users through subscriptions, ads, or in-app purchases. Consumers would ultimately pay more for access to apps that were previously free or affordable. Increased market concentration and unfair competition Google already dominates Android distribution through the Play Store. Making it the only permitted channel would cement this monopoly. Competing app stores and direct distribution models would be eliminated, reducing innovation and harming Europes digital market competitiveness. Exclusion of vulnerable or niche communities Some apps are made for highly specific communities (e.g. accessibility tools, regional cultural apps, or hobbyist tools). These may not meet Googles commercial standards or generate enough revenue to justify Play Store fees yet they provide important social and cultural value. Threat to privacy and security diversity Open-source apps often provide privacy-respecting alternatives to commercial products. By restricting distribution, users could lose access to these tools, leaving them more dependent on data-collecting, ad-driven services. Conflict with EU digital fairness principles Such a restriction would undermine consumer protection, reduce fairness, and diminish citizens ability to choose non-commercial, ethical software. It runs counter to the EUs goals of preventing unfair practices, avoiding addictive design, and ensuring a level playing field for digital services.
